participants.extracted: The quantitative evaluation included 4,468 older adults with complete baseline and follow-up data for mental health analyses, drawn from 516 localities in seven states of Mexico. The intervention group comprised adults aged 70–74 years living in localities with 2,500 or fewer inhabitants (n=1,353), compared with three control groups: adults aged 70–74 years in localities with 2,501–2,700 inhabitants (n=888), adults aged 65–69 years in localities with 2,500 or fewer inhabitants (n=1,345), and adults aged 65–69 years in localities with 2,501–2,700 inhabitants (n=882). For the qualitative component, the study included 129 semi-structured interviews with older adults (99 program beneficiaries, 16 potential beneficiaries, and 6 suspended beneficiaries), plus 8 interviews with local key actors and 4 observations of benefit delivery.

interventions.extracted: The intervention was Mexico’s non-contributory social pension program 70 y más. It provided older adults aged 70 years and older living in rural localities of 2,500 inhabitants or fewer with a direct unconditional cash transfer of 500 Mexican pesos per month, collectable every two months. The program was designed to improve living conditions by increasing income and social protection, and in this study the evaluated component was the cash transfer only.

outcomes.extracted: The study planned to measure mental well-being outcomes in older adults receiving the non-contributory pension program 70 y más, using baseline and 11–12 month follow-up assessments. The prespecified outcomes were: depressive symptoms, measured with the 15-item Geriatric Depression Scale (GDS-15) and operationalized as a binary indicator of significant depressive symptoms (GDS ≥ 6), at baseline and follow-up; participation in making household decisions, measured with a binary self-report indicator of whether the older adult participated in important non-economic household decisions, at baseline and follow-up; and participation in household spending decisions, measured with a binary self-report indicator of whether the older adult participated in decisions about household expenses, at baseline and follow-up.
